stem: Which agency is primarily responsible for certifying radioactive-material package designs, while another agency regulates labeling and routine transport?

options.text: The FDA certifies package designs; the NRC regulates labeling and transport
The Department of Transportation certifies package designs; the FDA regulates labeling and transport
The NRC certifies package designs; the Department of Transportation regulates labeling and transport
The NRC certifies package designs; the EPA regulates labeling and transport

explanation: The NRC reviews and certifies package designs for radioactive material. The Department of Transportation regulates transport requirements such as shipping papers, labels, and smaller packages. The FDA and EPA have other radiation-related roles but do not divide these transportation duties in this way.

stem: A shipment must withstand severe accident conditions, including impact, puncture, fire, and immersion. Which package type is required?

options.text: Excepted package
Industrial package
Type A package
Type B package

explanation: Type B packages are designed for high-activity contents and must withstand severe accident tests. Type A packages are designed for normal transport conditions and minor mishaps, not the full severe-accident sequence.

stem: What determines whether a radioactive shipment requires a Type A or Type B package?

options.text: The activity and form of the radioactive contents
The measured dose rate at 1 meter
The distance the shipment will travel
Whether the carrier is a common or private carrier

explanation: Package type is based principally on the activity and form of the radioactive material inside. By contrast, the external radiation hazard determines which category of package label is used.

stem: How is the transport index assigned to a radioactive package?

options.text: It is the surface dose rate in mrem per hour
It is the highest dose rate in mrem per hour measured 1 meter from the package
It is the package activity in millicuries divided by 10
It is the removable contamination level divided by the surface dose rate

explanation: The transport index is the highest radiation level, in mrem per hour, measured 1 meter from the package. It is used for spacing and transport controls. A Radioactive White-I package does not display a transport index.

stem: A package measures 0.4 mrem/hour at its surface and 0.2 mrem/hour at 1 meter. Which label is appropriate?

options.text: Radioactive Yellow-II
Radioactive Yellow-III
Radioactive White-I
No radioactive label is permitted

explanation: White-I is used when the surface dose rate is no more than 0.5 mrem/hour and the 1-meter dose rate is no more than 0.5 mrem/hour. Yellow-II and Yellow-III identify progressively greater external radiation hazards.

stem: A package measures 35 mrem/hour at its surface and has a transport index of 0.8. Which label is appropriate?

options.text: Radioactive White-I
Radioactive Yellow-III
Excepted quantity
Radioactive Yellow-II

explanation: Yellow-II applies when the surface dose rate is greater than 0.5 but no more than 50 mrem/hour and the transport index is no more than 1. Yellow-III is required if either the surface dose rate exceeds 50 mrem/hour or the transport index exceeds 1, within the applicable upper limits.

stem: A package has a surface dose rate of 40 mrem/hour and a dose rate of 2 mrem/hour at 1 meter. Which label is required?

options.text: Radioactive Yellow-III
Radioactive White-I
Radioactive Yellow-II
The package cannot be transported under any circumstances

explanation: Although the surface reading falls within the Yellow-II range, the 1-meter reading produces a transport index of 2. A transport index greater than 1 requires a Yellow-III label. Classification follows the more restrictive measurement.

stem: What is the usual maximum transport index for a package carried in a common-carrier vehicle or an open exclusive-use vehicle?
explanation: The usual maximum transport index is 10. A package with a transport index greater than 10 may be transported only under exclusive-use controls in a closed vehicle.

stem: Which information belongs on a radioactive package label?

options.text: Only the name and address of the recipient
The patient's name and medical record number
Radionuclide identity, activity, and transport index when applicable
The package purchase price and insurance value

explanation: Radioactive labels communicate the contents and external hazard, including radionuclide identity, quantity or activity, and transport index for Yellow-II and Yellow-III packages. Patient identifiers and financial information are not package-label requirements.

stem: Before an empty, uncontaminated shipping container is discarded as ordinary waste, what must be done?

options.text: The container must be stored for ten physical half-lives
The container must be returned to the NRC
A transport index of zero must be written on every label
All radioactive labels and markings must be removed or defaced

explanation: Radioactive labels and markings must be removed or defaced before an empty, uncontaminated package is released for unrestricted use or disposal. This prevents a nonradioactive package from being mistaken for a current radioactive shipment.

stem: A radioactive package arrives during normal working hours. By when must the required receipt surveys be completed?

options.text: Within 3 hours of receipt
Within 30 minutes
By the end of the calendar day
Within 24 hours

explanation: Required monitoring must be performed as soon as practical, but no later than 3 hours after receipt during working hours. If the package arrives after hours, the 3-hour period begins at the start of the next working day.

stem: A package of radioactive material is delivered after the nuclear medicine department has closed. When does the 3-hour monitoring period begin?

options.text: At the time the delivery vehicle enters the property
At the beginning of the next working day
At midnight
When the package is first opened

explanation: For an after-hours delivery, required monitoring must be completed within 3 hours after the beginning of the next working day. The clock does not wait until the package is opened.

stem: A receiving survey finds radiation or removable contamination above the applicable package limit. What is the appropriate immediate reporting action?

options.text: Notify only the prescribing authorized user within 30 days
Return the package without documenting the survey
Notify the carrier and the NRC or Agreement State immediately
Wait for a confirmatory annual audit before reporting

explanation: When package limits are exceeded, the licensee must immediately notify both the final delivery carrier and the NRC or Agreement State. Delayed internal review does not replace prompt external notification.

stem: A sealed source has no leak-test certificate documenting a test within the preceding 6 months. What should the licensee do?

options.text: Continue using it until the next annual inventory
Use it only for diagnostic procedures
Wipe the storage cabinet instead of the source
Remove it from use until it is tested

explanation: A source subject to leak testing may not be used or stored for use without a certificate showing a test within the preceding 6 months. It must be tested before returning to service.

stem: A sealed-source leak test detects 0.006 microcurie of removable contamination. What is required?

options.text: Remove the source from use and report the result within 5 days
No action because the result is below 0.01 microcurie
Repeat the test at the next 6-month interval
Relabel the source as unsealed material and continue using it

explanation: A leak-test result of at least 0.005 microcurie (185 Bq) is reportable. The source must be withdrawn from use and the leak reported within 5 days. The result in the scenario exceeds that threshold.

stem: How long must records of radiation surveys, instrument calibrations, and sealed-source leak tests generally be retained?

options.text: 1 year
3 years
5 years
For the duration of the license in every case

explanation: These operational records are generally retained for 3 years. Some other records, including specified individual monitoring and effluent records, have longer retention requirements, so the record category matters.

stem: When must an area survey be documented in a location where unsealed material requiring a written directive is prepared or administered?

options.text: Only after a spill
Once each calendar quarter
At the end of each day the material is used
Only when an employee dose exceeds an investigational level

explanation: The area must be surveyed at the end of each day on which unsealed byproduct material requiring a written directive is prepared or administered. The record includes the date, results, instrument, and person performing the survey.

stem: Which radioactive material may be discharged to a sanitary sewer under the routine disposal provision?

options.text: Any solid source with a half-life under 120 days
Any material released from a patient room
Only tritium, regardless of annual quantity
Material that is soluble or readily dispersible in water and remains within annual activity limits

explanation: Sewer disposal is limited to material that is soluble or readily dispersible in water and that remains within radionuclide-specific and total annual limits. Solid sealed sources do not qualify merely because they are short-lived.

stem: Which set gives the annual sanitary-sewer limits stated in the RISC document?

options.text: Tritium 5 Ci, carbon-14 1 Ci, and all other radionuclides combined 1 Ci
Tritium 1 Ci, carbon-14 5 Ci, and all other radionuclides combined 10 Ci
Tritium 10 Ci, carbon-14 10 Ci, and no combined limit for other radionuclides
One curie total for all radionuclides, with no isotope-specific limits

explanation: The annual limits are 5 Ci of tritium, 1 Ci of carbon-14, and 1 Ci for all other radionuclides combined. These limits do not eliminate the requirement that the material be soluble or readily dispersible.

stem: How are radioactive materials contained in a patient's urine or feces treated for sanitary-sewer disposal limits?

options.text: They are prohibited from entering a sanitary sewer
They are exempt from the sewer-disposal quantity limits
They count against the facility's annual radionuclide limits
They may be discharged only after ten physical half-lives

explanation: Radioactive material excreted by a patient is exempt from the routine sewer-disposal quantity limits. This patient-excreta exception should not be generalized to laboratory stock or other radioactive waste.

stem: Which waste is eligible for decay in storage under the RISC summary?

options.text: Material with a biological half-life of 120 days or less
Any material whose dose rate is below 2 mrem/hour
Material with a physical half-life of 120 days or less
Only material containing technetium-99m

explanation: Decay in storage may be used for material with a physical half-life of 120 days or less. Biological half-life describes clearance from an organism and does not determine eligibility of stored waste.

stem: Before decay-in-storage waste is released as ordinary trash, how should it be surveyed?

options.text: At 1 meter with the waste behind its storage shielding
Only by reviewing the calculated number of half-lives
With a personnel dosimeter placed beside it overnight
At the container surface with a sensitive instrument and no intervening shielding

explanation: The waste must be surveyed at the surface with an appropriate sensitive instrument, without shielding, and must be indistinguishable from background. A time calculation alone is not sufficient.

stem: What additional step is required after decay-in-storage waste is shown to be indistinguishable from background?

options.text: Remove or deface radiation labels before disposal
Add a new Radioactive White-I label
Send the waste to a low-level waste burial site
Retain it for another 120 days

explanation: Once the waste passes the required survey, radiation labels and markings must be removed or defaced before ordinary disposal. Survey records must be retained for 3 years.

stem: Which statement best distinguishes a sealed source from unsealed radioactive material?

options.text: A sealed source emits only gamma radiation
A sealed source is permanently bonded or encapsulated to prevent dispersion under normal use
A sealed source contains less than 0.005 microcurie
A sealed source never requires inventory or leak testing

explanation: A sealed source is constructed so radioactive material remains contained under normal conditions of use. The designation does not depend on radiation type or low activity, and many sealed sources require periodic inventory and leak testing.
